office:text.text:p: 1’.eden a du⁄⁄-ga íd-da mi-ni-Ñar-Ñar íd-da mi-ni-luh-luh

text:p.text:span: saying (the words) of Aradmu, your servant:
(Concerning) everything that my king commanded me (to do):
(The entire kingdom), from the sea of Dilmun land
to the brackish waters at the foot of the Amorite Highlands,
to the borders of Simurum in the land of Subir—
I have inspected the various cities, their (surrounding) territories, their fields and agricultural tracts, and their levees and irrigation ditches; I have imprisoned (all) fugitives.
I have established a lookout for any leaks
All the cities obedient to my king
I strengthened the garrisons on their walls;
I made their troops submit (to you).
s, and canebrakes in the marshes …
Wherever the drawing of water was not sufficient (to irrigate gardens), I had … pour water by means of buckets.
On the irrigated desert they set it in watercourses, they wash in the watercourses.
Even rustlers and robbers break up the earth (for cultivation) on the (wind)-swept desert.
Alternative translation: I have made rustlers and robbers break up the earth (for cultivation) on the wind-swept steppe.
As for their men and women, their men go wherever they wish,
and their women hold spindles and­ needless, wandering on whatever roads they wish.
They set up animal pens in the far-flung desert,
and after pitching their tents and encampments,
Var.: They rest safely in their tents and encampments.
their laborers and tenant farmers then spend their days (working) in the­ fields.
I have not neglected the orders of
I resisted but now I attend to those orders (without sleep) at night or during the midday siesta!
Apilaåa, from childhood, has been loyal to me.
And when he became a (grown) man
, I recognized him in my heart, and once it was clear to you,
my king, with divine insight, you recognized that person,
and promoted him above all the (other) high
My king, you surpass all great deeds, you [take revenge] on my opponents.
Your orders are imperative orders; your deeds are great deeds!
(Apilaåa’s) supplications are in your heart, daily they are.
He is the mortal who has found favor in your eyes,
so how could I ever be set against him?
I have secured the foundations of the frontier and made obedient (all of its inhabitants).
No king can rival my king!
In view of my reconstruction of CBS 7787+ (source N1 of this composition), it would appear that what has been thought of as two separate letters
are actually one, and constitute a reply to
Ar1. The reconstruction of the tablet is discussed on p.
above. There is no overlap between the two preserved sections but there seem to be between two to five lines missing.

office:text.text:p: Although the composition is relatively well attested, and was clearly part of the regular set of CKU letters at Nippur, the reconstruction of the text is still uncertain, and must be considered preliminary until better preserved sources are recovered. Many difficulties remain. Note also that this is the only CKU text excerpted for early scribal training on a lentil outside of Susa (source Si1).

text:p.text:span: 70 (2001) 313–17, who opts for a rendition “(communally held) meadow.” It is clear from numerous references in administrative and literary texts alike that the
was cultivated, and cannot be a meadow. Therefore I am inclined to follow Stol (“irrigated fields”).
The translation follows the meaning of
68–69 (“to irrigate, to be submerged”), so that the literal meaning would be, “have caused the submerged fields to come out.” Just recently, W. Heimpel,
Workers and Construction Work in Garåana
277–78 claims that it means “to set/prepare for watering.” While I find much of his argument convincing, it does not fit the context here.
is used in the sense of Akkadian
occurs otherwise only once, in a Rim-Sin royal inscription (RS I 8:43 [E4.2.14.20], in conjunction with a wall); it seems to be a calque from the Akkadian use of
M/1 335. Line 9 is more difficult. First, the verb
, “to inspect, investigate,” is followed by what seems to be
is not there in N6 and the whole complex is missing in N2. Here
. To my knowledge, the only other example of this combination is in an Ur III letter-order from Girsu,

, “it was (PN) who detained him for me by command of the prison (warden) on my behalf” (translation uncertain). With reservations, I have assumed that the verb /ed/ refers to escaped soldiers and workers, who have all been caught and detained.
is difficult. The approximate translation is based on
115 (“not in use,” but note Civil’s comments on the philological difficulties of this section of Hh).
. The translation of these lines is provisional at best. As W. Heimpel
Workers and Construction Work in Garåana
refers to pouring water, not to
as “deficit,” or the like, follows K. R. Veenhof,
. This line would only make sense if we knew what preceded it; the direct objects must have been in the lines above.
. The lack of preceding context hinders the interpretation of these lines as well. Presumably the description of lawless men and women settling down, building encampments, and raising crops on the
(“desert, wilderness;” often rendered as “plain”), that is outside of the normal agricultural areas, must be related to
Ar1: 13–16 (3). Presumably Aradmu is explaining why he had taken time on his journey to Apilaåa to inspect the
. Thus, the passage concludes with the words (lines 8’–9’): “I have not neglected the orders of
ulgi, my king! I resistedbut now I attend to these orders night and day!”
is rendered in lexical texts by
12 166]). The Sumerian is more specific, however; W. Heimpel (
8 [1995] 106–7) has suggested that it is the word for “cattle rustler.”
Note also the literary of danger from brigands/rustlers in the desert:
, “Scorpions and rustlers, creatures of the ­desert, threaten your life in the high desert,” with variant

office:text.text:p: Although the immediate context is missing, it would seem that the lines that follow pertain to these unruly people. Therefore, Aradmu seems to be reassuring the king that all is so well that even rustlers and brigands have settled down—or have been settled by him—and are involved with taming the land and agriculture.

text:p.text:span: The meaning of the line is complicated by
, which I take to be an abbreviation or corruption of the rare
im hul-hul im si-si-ig dù-a-bi ur-bi ì-su
, “all the sweeping gales were gathered together.” More important is the parallel line in
lú-sa-gaz-e edin im si-si-ga níÑ-gul-bi hu-mu-ak
I have made bandits break up the earth (for cultivation) on the wind-swept desert” (in broken context; others have interpreted this passage differently).
. These lines are repeated in letter
ÅPu1 MB1 9–14 (letter 14). For lines 5’–6’ see the commentary to that letter below.
seems to be a traditional metaphor for aspects of femininity (see, for example,
seems well established, the meaning of
) is problematical; it is usually rendered as “hair clasp,” but W. Farber,
98, argues for “needle.” Both are traditional accoutrements symbolizing femininity in Mesopotamian literary texts; for wider ancient Near Easter use of the spindle trope, see H. A. Hoffner,
is somewhat hazy. The former occurs only once outside of CKU, in a Sin-iddinam inscription [no. 6, E.: 33], and the latter is quite rare. The rendering of
as “tenant farmer, cultivator,” follows P. Steinkeller,

. The interpretation of this line is hindered by the state of preservation of the witnesses. Only one Nippur manuscript fully preserves the beginning of the line (N4); X2 is problematic and seems to bear the traces of textual corruption and reinterpretation. Note,
in that source, the use of
, which is otherwise unattested in OB literary texts, although it is known from administrative documents.
, which is often translated “to neglect, despise,” (F. Karahashi,
95) but which carries a more nuanced meaning “to be remiss, to turn against, resist,” may be a better rendering. Note that it is used of rebellious kings in the inscriptions of Samsu-iluna.
, “to apply oneself to a task,” is also encountered with somewhat similar usage in
, “Don’t (idly) pass the day, do not (even) relax at night—apply yourself to that task!”
. This is the only example known to date of a finite form of the rare verb
. It is otherwise known only attested in the expression
, “the poor are loyal” (SP 9 Sec. A 2, B. Alster,
2 104 also argues for the meaning “to become rich, to prosper,” for the verb, but that does not seem to fit the context here. Note M. Civil’s rendering,
as “one who keeps tight control, energetic.”
is rare. The assumption here is that the verbal form is derived from the copula “to be” and is the same as
2003: 6 line 18, and unpublished examples). The temporal use of
has been discussed by M. Civil,
. The restoration of the verb as
, “to take revenge,” follows a suggestion by Steve Tinney.

office:text.text:p: 18’. This line links the text with ŠAr1: 27 (letter 2) and suggests that it is an answer to that letter.

text:p.text:span: -like passage, which is more reminiscent of letter-prayers than of prose literary letters.
The surface of this tablet is much worn. Unless there are parts of a sign clearly missing, recognizable signs are marked by +.
Lentil with one line of the text distributed over three lines (same text on obverse and reverse).

office:text.text:p: Tablet typology: compilation tablets: N1, X1. The rest are all Type III, except Si, which is a type IV lentil.
Photograph of Si1 by Gary Beckman.
N3 -+ + o o o o o o

text:p.text:span: N3 + - + + . o o o o o o o o o

office:text.text:p: N3 + . . o o o o o

text:p.text:span: N3 o . o o o o o
N2 o o o . - - + + + zu . x
11. ki x x a-bi ì-KU šu-úr im šu bí
N5 ki x x a-bi ì-KU šu-úr im šu bí
X1 [] x x [] im šu bí-x
N1 o o o o + + + . o

office:text.text:p: X1 o o o o + o o o .
N2 o o o o . + + + + + x

text:p.text:span: o o o o o o o o o o o o
X2 o o . + + + i + - + ì-dun ì - + .
N1 + + + o o o o o o o o o o o o o + +
N1 + + - + + . o o o o o o o o
kirid-da šu ba-e-si-ig har-ra-an­ šà-ga-na du-ù-ne
N1 + + + + + . o o o o o o + + + + + o o o o
o o o o o o
N1 . + + + . o o o o o o
N7 o o o o . + + . o o o

N1 + + + . o o o o o o o o o o
+ x o o o o o o o o o o o

. o o o o o o o o o o o
N6 oo o o o o + + + . + .
N7 oo o o o . . . o o
o o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: Lentil with the same text inscribed on obverse and reverse, by the teacher and then by the student. The line is entered as a composite of both sides. It is difficult to ascertain if the root of the verb is the final sign, or if there was something after that/

text:p.text:span: N1 + + + + + o o o o o o o o o

text:note-body.text:p: The scribe was running out of space in the last line of the obverse and wrote what looks like igi-bar for igi-zu, probably also thinking of ù, resulting in some confusion. He then wrote the line again on the reverse of the tablet, attempting to correct himself, but changing the predicate. Were two different versions available to him?

text:p.text:span: 1 . + + + . o o o o o o o o o

office:text.text:p: N1 o o o . . . + . . o o o

text:p.text:span: X2 . . + . . o o o o o o x .
